title: In re Tesla Autopilot Litigation — consolidated product liability class action
summary: Consolidated class action alleging Tesla's Autopilot and Full Self-Driving systems are defective and that Tesla misrepresented their capabilities to consumers. Plaintiffs allege injuries arising from collisions and near-misses attributable to system failures.

title: Beijing Consumers Association v. Zhejiang Geely Holding Group — battery fire disclosure claim

parties.defendant: Zhejiang Geely Holding Group Co., Ltd.

summary: Consumer advocacy organization alleges Geely failed to timely disclose known battery thermal runaway defects in the Galaxy E8 model, resulting in underreporting of fire incidents to the market regulator. Claims violation of the Consumer Rights Protection Law Art. 20 and the Product Quality Law Art. 27.

title: Ford Motor Co. v. Contemporary Amperex Technology Co. (CATL) — trade secret misappropriation re LFP cell manufacturing
summary: Ford alleges CATL misappropriated proprietary manufacturing processes for lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells developed during their joint BlueOval SK battery venture. Parallel proceedings filed in both E.D. Michigan and Shenzhen IP Court. Case raises novel questions of cross-border trade secret enforcement and choice of law.

title: Li v. BYD Auto Co., Ltd. — lemon law claim, range degradation after OTA software update
summary: Plaintiff alleged that BYD's OTA update reduced the Han EV's rated range by 18% without consumer consent or adequate disclosure, constituting a product defect under China's Three-Package Rules for Automobiles. Court awarded full refund plus 10% punitive supplement under the Consumer Rights Protection Law Art. 55.

title: State of California v. Volkswagen Group of America, Inc. — residual emissions fraud enforcement
summary: California AG alleges Volkswagen continued to deploy defeat device logic in MY2022-2024 turbocharged ICE vehicles sold in California, in violation of the state's 2020 consent decree arising from the Dieselgate settlement. Action seeks civil penalties of $37,500 per violation under the California Clean Air Act and Health and Safety Code Section 43016.

title: Wang v. NIO, Inc. — sudden unintended acceleration resulting in highway collision
summary: Plaintiff alleges NIO's ES8 accelerated uncontrollably at highway speeds due to a software defect in the drive control unit, causing a rear-end collision with personal injury. NIO disputes the characterization and points to driver error data from on-board telemetry. Case involves competing expert testimony on event data recorder interpretation.

title: Rivian Automotive, Inc. v. Scout Motors Inc. — trade dress infringement, vehicle design
summary: Rivian alleges Scout's Terra pickup truck and Traveler SUV designs improperly copy Rivian's distinctive front lamp design and overall vehicle silhouette trade dress, creating consumer confusion in the electric truck market. Motion to dismiss pending; Scout argues trade dress is functional and lacks secondary meaning.

title: Chen v. SAIC-GM-Wuling Automobile — airbag non-deployment, Hongguang MINI EV
summary: Plaintiff sustained serious injuries in a frontal collision when airbags failed to deploy in the Hongguang MINI EV at speeds triggering deployment per specifications. Court found product defect under China's Product Quality Law Art. 41, ordering compensation of CNY 340,000. Defendant has appealed to the Guangxi Higher People's Court.

title: Lu Hui v. Mitsubishi Motors Corporation (Japan) et al. – Brake Failure Personal Injury (Trademark Counterfeiting)
court: Furong District People's Court, Changsha City, Hunan Province
status: Decided – Li Zhiming liable; Mitsubishi dismissed; appeal dismissed, original judgment upheld
summary: On December 25, 2000, defendant Li Zhiming was operating a 'Sanleng' brand SUV in Changsha when the vehicle's rear braking system failed, causing a collision with plaintiff Lu Hui, a pedestrian. Lu Hui sustained a fractured skull, severe traumatic brain injury, and total physical disability (Grade 1). The Hunan Provincial Bureau of Technical Supervision determined that brake oil pipe wear and rupture caused hydraulic fluid loss, resulting in complete brake failure. The subject vehicle was a 'GS Sanleng SKD Jeep' assembled by Hunan Hanshou Vehicle Modification Factory and purchased by Li Zhiming in December 1996. The vehicle bore the Mitsubishi trademark without authorization, having been assembled and distributed without Mitsubishi Motors Corporation's knowledge or consent. Defendant Mitsubishi advanced CNY 120,000 to the treating hospital on humanitarian grounds, expressly disclaiming any admission of liability. Following investigation, Mitsubishi determined the vehicle was not a lawfully imported genuine Mitsubishi product. The court of first instance held that any party affixing its name or trademark to a product is presumed its manufacturer, but that no liability attaches where the mark was applied by a third party through counterfeiting. Finding no evidentiary nexus between the subject vehicle and Mitsubishi Motors Corporation, the court dismissed all claims against Mitsubishi and apportioned liability between Li Zhiming (CNY 383,027) and Lu Hui (residual loss of CNY 547,181 total). Both parties appealed; the Changsha Intermediate People's Court dismissed the appeals and affirmed the judgment in full.

title: Zhang Xuyang v. Shenyang Aokesi Automobile Co., Ltd. et al. – Illegally Produced Vehicle, Rescission of Purchase Contract
status: Decided (first instance) – defendants liable; appeal resolved by mediated settlement

parties.defendant: Shenyang Aokesi Automobile Co., Ltd.; Ningbo Aokesi Automobile Co., Ltd.; Aokesi Group Co., Ltd.; Shenyang Fusang Heidao Co., Ltd.

summary: In March 2005, plaintiff Zhang Xuyang purchased an Aokesi Langjie SUV for CNY 73,800, manufactured by Shenyang Aokesi Automobile Co., Ltd. and distributed by Ningbo Aokesi. Following Aokesi Group's market withdrawal in April 2005, plaintiff discovered that Shenyang Aokesi lacked a lawful vehicle production qualification and had assembled the vehicle under Fusang Heidao's production license without state approval, constituting unlawful vehicle assembly. Plaintiff sought rescission of the purchase contract, full refund, and compensation for registration costs of CNY 10,117.53, asserting joint and several liability against all four defendants on grounds of license lending, trademark lending, and general distributorship. The court of first instance ordered return of the vehicle, refund of the purchase price, and compensation of economic losses, with Fusang Heidao bearing joint and several liability for the monetary obligations. Three defendants appealed to the Dalian Intermediate People's Court; the matter was resolved by judicial mediation.

title: Mr. Cai & Ms. Huang v. DaimlerChrysler et al. – Airbag Non-Deployment and Seatbelt Failure Causing Death (Mercedes-Benz S320)
court: Dongguan Intermediate People's Court, Guangdong Province

parties.defendant: DaimlerChrysler Co., Ltd.; Liang Zhiwei; He Zhiwei

summary: On January 31, 1999, Cai Moupeng, eldest son of the plaintiffs, was killed when his Mercedes-Benz S320 struck a guardrail on the Guangshen Expressway (Humen section), fell into a roadside ditch, and both front airbags failed to deploy while the driver's seatbelt fractured. Traffic authorities confirmed the passive safety system failures. Plaintiffs brought suit in the Dongguan Intermediate People's Court alleging product defect causation, seeking apology, compensation, and litigation costs. DaimlerChrysler denied causation; defendants Liang Zhiwei and He Zhiwei, shareholders of intermediary seller Hong Kong Furong Company, defaulted. The court found a legally cognizable causal nexus between the safety system failures and the decedent's death, ordering all defendants jointly to pay approximately CNY 90,000 in compensatory damages, and DaimlerChrysler to pay each plaintiff CNY 100,000 in emotional distress damages (CNY 200,000 total), with the two seller-defendants bearing joint and several liability.
